topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red ink on the paper seemed to vibrate under my gaze. A C-minus. It was a significant drop from the A I’d been anticipating, a result of hours spent staring blankly at the textbook, the words swimming before my eyes like disturbed water. I dragged my chair back, the scrape of the metal legs against the linoleum sounding unnervingly loud in the quiet study room. My eyelids felt weighted, heavy anchors pulling me down. I picked up my pen, the plastic cool and unresponsive in my fingers, and found myself just tracing the edge of the paper, the effort required to form a coherent thought feeling monumental. *Why?* the thought echoed hollowly in the space behind my ribs. I leaned my head against the cool glass of the window, watching the afternoon sun streak across the dusty floor, wishing the inertia of the afternoon would simply lift me.

[story 2]

“Well, that’s just… disappointing,” I mumbled, the words tasting dry and unfamiliar on my tongue. The professor’s feedback, delivered with a clipped, impersonal tone, landed like a physical blow. I shuffled my feet beneath the desk, a slow, deliberate movement that seemed to take an immense amount of energy. Every muscle felt disconnected, operating on some distant, slow-motion track. I stared at the equation circled in angry red, unable to summon the mental gears to even begin dissecting the error. My throat felt tight, as if I’d swallowed sand. I pushed my laptop away, the screen’s bright light suddenly too intense, and just let my gaze drift toward the ceiling tiles, counting the faint, rhythmic patterns, hoping some kind of external stimulation might jolt me back into motion.

[story 3]

I reread the grading rubric three times, the paper blurring slightly at the edges. The sheer volume of missed concepts pressed down on me, creating a dense, suffocating pressure behind my sternum. It wasn't just the grade; it was the feeling of having poured everything I had into the work, only to watch it evaporate into mediocrity. I slowly reached for my water bottle, the plastic cool against my palm, and took a long, slow sip, the liquid doing little to clear the fog in my mind. My limbs felt heavy, resistant to any impulse toward action. I sighed, a deep, drawn-out sound that seemed to expel all the remaining vitality from my chest, and settled back into the chair, letting the quiet, heavy realization settle over me like a thick blanket.
